---
title: "Building My Clinic Blog as Code: Quarto, GitHub Pages, and Google Analytics"
description: >
  Kicking off the Digital Summer Clinic by treating my blog like a software
  project — and instrumenting it with analytics from the very first post.
date: "2026-06-22"
categories: [Digital Summer Clinic, Quarto, Analytics, Web]
---

This is the start of a weekly habit. Over the next nine weeks of the **2026
Digital Summer Clinic**, I'll be working with a startup on its digital marketing,
studying for the **Google Analytics certification**, and writing here every week
about what I'm doing and learning. First entry: the blog itself.

## Why not just use Blogger?

The clinic recommends Blogger — and it's a perfectly good, free choice. I went a
different route on purpose. My core strengths are **coding** and **analytics**, so
I wanted the way I publish to be evidence of that, not just a place to talk about
it. Instead of a hosted editor, this site is a small software project:

- **Quarto** turns plain Markdown into a clean, fast website. No database, no
  plugins to keep patched — just text files I can version and diff.
- **GitHub** stores the source. Every change is a commit with a message, so the
  blog has a full history.
- **GitHub Actions** rebuilds and deploys the site automatically every time I
  push. I never click "publish" — `git push` *is* publishing.
- **GitHub Pages** hosts it for free at
  [mattzhou98.github.io/blog](https://mattzhou98.github.io/blog).

Writing a post is now the same motion as shipping code: edit a Markdown file,
commit, push, and ~60 seconds later it's live.

## Measuring from day one

The clinic asks us to track our blogs with **Google Analytics**, and that lines up
neatly with the certification I'm studying for. Rather than bolt analytics on
later, I wired **Google Analytics 4** in before the first reader ever arrived,
with a lightweight cookie-consent banner so it's transparent about tracking.

Practicing GA on something I actually own — watching which posts get read, where
readers come from, how the LinkedIn → blog link performs — is a much better way to
learn the tool than studying it in the abstract. That's the whole idea behind the
clinic's "blog + analytics" assignment: use the act of marketing *yourself* as the
sandbox for learning to market anything.

## What's coming

Each week I'll post about one of three things:

1. **The work** — what I'm building for my SPARK incubator company (in general
   terms where I need to respect confidentiality).
2. **The certification** — what I'm learning about Google Analytics, dimensions
   and metrics, events, and attribution.
3. **The reading** — interesting things from industry sources like eMarketer,
   Moz, and AdAge that change how I think about digital.

If you're a recruiter, a fellow intern, or just curious — welcome. New post every
week. Next up: setting the foundation with my SPARK company.
